questions.q: In the Puranas, what does the term 'pralaya' refer to?
options: The birth of the first sage
questions.explanation: Pralaya is the periodic dissolution of the created worlds described in the Puranas.

questions.q: According to Puranic cosmology, the worlds undergo pralaya at the close of which span?
questions.explanation: Pralaya occurs at the close of a cosmic age, when the created worlds are reabsorbed.

questions.q: In Hindu cosmology, pralaya (dissolution) is paired with which complementary cosmic process?
questions.explanation: Pralaya, the dissolution, alternates with srishti, the creation of the worlds.

questions.q: The Puranas describe the greatest dissolution at the end of the life of which creator deity?
questions.explanation: The maha-pralaya occurs at the end of Brahma's life, after which all returns to the unmanifest.

questions.q: After a pralaya, according to the Puranas, the cosmos returns to what state until a new cycle begins?
questions.explanation: After dissolution all returns to the unmanifest until a new cycle of creation begins.

questions.q: Which element is most often associated with the imagery of the final dissolution in Puranic accounts of pralaya?
questions.explanation: Puranic accounts vividly depict a cosmic fire that consumes the worlds during dissolution.

questions.q: In Puranic teaching, the cycle of creation and dissolution is described as which kind of process?
questions.explanation: The Puranas present creation and dissolution as a recurring, cyclical rhythm of the cosmos.

questions.q: The doctrine of pralaya, the dissolution of the worlds, is chiefly elaborated in which body of texts?
questions.explanation: The concept of pralaya is chiefly elaborated in the Puranas.

questions.q: In the famous Puranic image linked to dissolution, which deity reclines in cosmic slumber on the serpent Shesha between cycles?
questions.explanation: Vishnu as Narayana reclines in yoga nidra upon Shesha between the cycles of creation.

questions.q: Traditions describe both lesser and greater dissolutions; the very greatest is given which name?
questions.explanation: The greatest dissolution, at the end of Brahma's life, is called the maha-pralaya.

questions.q: In Puranic cosmology, the dissolution that occurs at the end of one of Brahma's days is commonly called which type of pralaya?
questions.explanation: The dissolution at the close of a day of Brahma is traditionally termed the naimittika or occasional pralaya.

questions.q: The Puranas measure a vast cosmic age as a 'kalpa,' which is described as equivalent to what in Brahma's reckoning?
questions.explanation: A kalpa is described in the Puranas as a single day of Brahma, ending in dissolution.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, a complete cosmic cycle is divided into four ages (yugas); the last and most diminished is named what?
questions.explanation: The Kali Yuga is the last of the four yugas, after which the cycle eventually turns toward dissolution and renewal.

questions.q: In Puranic accounts of the dissolution, which avatar of Vishnu is said to preserve the seeds of life through a great deluge?
questions.explanation: In the Matsya story, Vishnu as the fish guides Manu's boat and preserves life through the cosmic flood.

questions.q: In the Puranic story of the great flood, the sage-king whom Matsya guides to safety is traditionally named whom?
questions.explanation: Matsya guides Manu's boat through the deluge, preserving the seeds of creation for the next age.

questions.q: When the worlds are dissolved at pralaya, from whose navel-lotus is the new creation said to begin in Puranic imagery?
questions.explanation: From the lotus rising out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma is born to begin a new creation.

questions.q: In Puranic accounts, the dissolution that ends Brahma's entire lifespan, returning even the elements to their source, is which type?
questions.explanation: The prakritika pralaya, at the end of Brahma's life, dissolves the very elements back into primal nature.

questions.q: In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna teaches Arjuna that at the close of a cosmic age all beings return into which divine source?
questions.explanation: In the Gita, Krishna says that at the end of an age all beings merge into his prakriti and are sent forth again at the new creation.

questions.q: The Puranas describe Vishnu's restful state upon the cosmic waters between dissolution and creation by which term?
questions.explanation: Vishnu reclines in yoga nidra, cosmic slumber, upon the serpent between the cycles of creation.

questions.q: On which serpent is Vishnu depicted reclining upon the cosmic ocean during the interval following dissolution?
questions.explanation: Vishnu reclines upon the thousand-headed serpent Shesha, also called Ananta, on the cosmic waters.

questions.q: Which goddess is described as attending at Vishnu's feet as he reclines between the cosmic cycles of dissolution and creation?
questions.explanation: Lakshmi attends at Vishnu's feet as he reclines on the serpent between the cycles.

questions.q: In Puranic reckoning, the recurring framework of creation and dissolution is governed by the lifespan of which deity in particular?
questions.explanation: The Puranas tie the rhythm of creation and dissolution to the days, nights, and lifespan of Brahma.

questions.q: Puranic tradition counts four kinds of dissolution; which term names the 'absolute' dissolution understood as the liberation of an individual soul?
questions.explanation: Atyantika pralaya refers to the absolute dissolution, the final liberation (moksha) of the individual soul.

questions.q: Among the four kinds of dissolution in Puranic thought, 'nitya pralaya' refers to which constant phenomenon?
options: The flooding of the seven oceans
The continual perishing of beings each moment
questions.explanation: Nitya pralaya is the constant, perpetual dissolution seen in the moment-to-moment perishing of living beings.

questions.q: In Puranic descriptions of the great dissolution, a fearsome cosmic fire is often said to issue from which serpent-associated source?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe the Samvartaka, a cosmic conflagration associated with the serpent, that scorches the worlds at dissolution.

questions.q: The naimittika (occasional) pralaya at the end of a day of Brahma is followed by which corresponding period of rest?
questions.explanation: The occasional dissolution at the end of a day of Brahma is followed by an equally long night of Brahma before re-creation.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, during the prakritika dissolution the manifest universe is reabsorbed ultimately into which primordial principle?
questions.explanation: In the prakritika pralaya the cosmos is reabsorbed into prakriti, the unmanifest primal nature, until the next creation.

questions.q: In the Bhagavad Gita's teaching on cosmic time, the recurring 'day' at whose dawning all beings emerge and at whose close they dissolve belongs to whom?
questions.explanation: The Gita speaks of the day and night of Brahma, at whose dawn beings emerge and at whose dusk they dissolve.

questions.q: Puranic cosmology often groups one thousand cycles of the four yugas into a single unit that equals one day of Brahma; this unit is called what?
questions.explanation: A kalpa, comprising a thousand cycles of the four yugas, equals one day of Brahma and ends in dissolution.

questions.q: In the Matsya account of the deluge that precedes a new age, the sage Manu is instructed to gather and preserve which things upon the boat?
options: The seeds of all creatures and the sacred knowledge
questions.explanation: Matsya bids Manu carry the seeds of all living beings and the sacred Vedic knowledge through the flood to seed the next creation.
